EDCS-13200222.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Curculio, indicating his name and age at death.

EDCS-13200223.translation: To the Divine Manes. D. Romana lived 61 years.
EDCS-13200223.summary: This is a funerary inscription for D. Romana, recording her name and age at death.

EDCS-13200224.translation: To the Divine Manes. Decumia lived 85 years. Everything helps (?).
EDCS-13200224.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Decumia, providing her age and a partially legible, somewhat unusual concluding phrase.

EDCS-13200225.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucretia Hospitilla lived 46 years. May your bones rest well. To the Divine Manes. Iulia Maura, a very rare and beautiful girl, lived 31 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-13200225.summary: This is a double funerary inscription for Lucretia Hospitilla and Iulia Maura, describing their names, ages, and including a special commendation for Iulia Maura, with wishes for peaceful rest.

EDCS-13200227.translation: Quintus Domitius Rogatus lived 80 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13200227.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Quintus Domitius Rogatus, who lived to an old age, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200228.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Quintus Domitius Saturninus, a most sweet and incomparable son, lived 23 years. May your bones rest well here.
EDCS-13200228.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits, honoring Quintus Domitius Saturninus, a young man commemorated by his family, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200229.translation: To the memory of Domitia Marcellina, a most rare daughter. She lived 1 year, 5 months, 8 days. Domitius Aprilis, her father, set this up.
EDCS-13200229.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Domitius Aprilis for his exceptionally young daughter, Domitia Marcellina, who died as an infant, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200230.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Domitia Mussa, Domitius Marcellinus, her father, made this. She lived one year and one month.
EDCS-13200230.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits, made by Domitius Marcellinus for his very young daughter, Domitia Mussa, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200232.translation: Publius Dupidius Agathopus lived 101 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13200232.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Publius Dupidius Agathopus, who lived to an exceptionally old age, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200233.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Publius Dupidius Saxa lived 111 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13200233.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits, commemorating Publius Dupidius Saxa, who lived to a remarkable age, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200234.summary: This is a concise funerary inscription for Dupidia Fausta, stating her age at death, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200235.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Dionysius lived 61 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13200235.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits, recording the burial of Dionysius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200236.translation: Quintus Egrilius Clemens, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, lived 35 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13200236.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Egrilius Clemens, identifying his father and tribe, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200237.translation: Gaius Egrilius Cursor lived 80 years and 10 months and a half.
EDCS-13200237.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating the age at death of Gaius Egrilius Cursor, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200238.translation: To the Divine Manes. Egrilius Hilario lived 45 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-13200238.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Egrilius Hilario at the age of 45.

EDCS-13200239.translation: To the Divine Manes. Epictesis lived 30 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-13200239.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Epictesis at the age of 30.

EDCS-13200240.translation: To the Divine Manes. Euphemus lived 90 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-13200240.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Euphemus at the age of 90.

EDCS-13200242.translation: To the Divine Manes. Quintus Fabius Cronta lived 15 years.
EDCS-13200242.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Quintus Fabius Cronta at the age of 15.

EDCS-13200243.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Fabius Victor lived 19 years.
EDCS-13200243.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Lucius Fabius Victor at the age of 19.

EDCS-13200244.translation: To the Divine Manes. Fabia Issula lived 25 years.
EDCS-13200244.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Fabia Issula at the age of 25.

EDCS-13200245.translation: To the Divine Manes. Fabricia Aprilia lived 59 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-13200245.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Fabricia Aprilia at the age of 59.

EDCS-13200246.translation: To the Divine Manes. Faosa(?) lived 35 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-13200246.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Faosa at the age of 35.

EDCS-13200247.translation: Lucius Farsuleius Faustus lived 14 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-13200247.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Lucius Farsuleius Faustus at the age of 14.

EDCS-13200248.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Felicia at the age of 30.

EDCS-13200249.translation: To the Divine Manes. Festulus lived for 20 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200249.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Festulus, recording his age at death and place of burial, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13200251.translation: Lucius Flavius Vicus lived for 80 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200251.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Lucius Flavius Vicus, stating his age and burial place, indicating it is an epitaph for an individual.

EDCS-13200252.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Flavius Vitulus lived for 25 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200252.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Vitulus, dedicating the monument to the Divine Manes and noting his age at death and burial location.

EDCS-13200253.translation: To the Divine Manes, sacred. Flavia Rogata, daughter of Lucius, lived for 77 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-13200253.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Flavia Rogata, daughter of Lucius, specifying her age at death and burial place, dedicated to the Divine Manes.

EDCS-13200256.translation: To the Divine Manes. Fonteia Vernalis lived for 71 and a half years. She is buried here.
EDCS-13200256.summary: This funerary inscription honors Fonteia Vernalis, dedicating the monument to the Divine Manes and providing her precise age at death and burial spot.

EDCS-13200257.translation: Fortunata lived for 40 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-13200257.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Fortunata, indicating her age at death and that she is interred at this spot.

EDCS-13200258.translation: To the Divine Manes. Quintus Fullonius Iucundianus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, a good man, lived for 46 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200258.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Divine Manes for Quintus Fullonius Iucundianus, a citizen of the Quirina tribe, noting his paternity, moral character, age, and burial location.

EDCS-13200259.translation: Lucius Fullonius Macer, son of Lucius...
EDCS-13200259.summary: This is an incomplete inscription for Lucius Fullonius Macer, son of Lucius, likely a funerary or dedicatory monument, but the full content is lost.

EDCS-13200261.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Furfanius Forticulus, recording his young age at death, indicating a child's epitaph.

EDCS-13200262.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Gabidius Gaiolus lived for 60 years.
EDCS-13200262.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Divine Manes for Marcus Gabidius Gaiolus, stating his age at death.

EDCS-13200263.translation: To Gabinia Lucula, who lived 101 years. She is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-13200263.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gabinia Lucula, likely a woman of advanced age, wishing her bones to rest well.

EDCS-13200264.translation: Gaius Gargilius Caninianus, son of Gaius, lived 15 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200264.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Gargilius Caninianus, a young man who died at the age of fifteen.

EDCS-13200265.translation: Lucius Gargilius Proculus lived 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200265.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Gargilius Proculus, recording his age and burial place.

EDCS-13200266.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Gaudianus Victor, son of Lucius, lived 67 years. He is buried here. May his bones rest well.
EDCS-13200266.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, commemorating Lucius Gaudianus Victor and wishing him peaceful rest.

EDCS-13200267.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Gavius Publicianus lived 61 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200267.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Marcus Gavius Publicianus.

EDCS-13200268.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Gellius Potitus lived 20 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200268.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Gellius Potitus, a young man who died at the age of twenty.

EDCS-13200269.translation: To the Divine Manes. Gellia Daphne lived 101 years.
EDCS-13200269.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Gellia Daphne, a woman who lived to a very old age.

EDCS-13200272.translation: To the Divine Manes. Geminius Ianuarius, son of Heliodorus, lived 30 years. To the Divine Manes. Heliodorus lived 105 years. He is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-13200272.summary: This is a double funerary inscription, dedicating monuments to Geminius Ianuarius, who died at 30, and his father Heliodorus, who lived to 105, both wishing for peaceful rest.

EDCS-13200273.translation: To the Divine Manes. Gaius Geminius Secundus lived 80 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13200273.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Gaius Geminius Secundus, recording his age and burial.

EDCS-13200274.translation: To the Divine Manes. Gemini VE[---]
EDCS-13200274.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for someone named Gemini, with the rest of the text lost.

EDCS-13200275.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Geminia Candida lived for 25 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-13200275.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Geminia Candida, who lived 25 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period, wishing her bones eternal rest.

EDCS-13200276.translation: Geminia Fortunata lived for 21 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13200276.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription for Geminia Fortunata, who died at the age of 21, indicating her burial place.

EDCS-13200277.translation: To the Divine Manes. Geminia Ingenua, a woman of one husband, a most sweet co-preserver, mother of all people, a parent helping everyone, innocent, most chaste, outstanding, most rare. She lived 81 years. She made no one sad. May her bones rest well.
EDCS-13200277.summary: This is a detailed funerary inscription dedicated to Geminia Ingenua, extolling her virtues as a chaste, helpful, and kind woman who lived to be 81, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13200278.translation: Geminia Matrona, daughter of Aulus, lived for 120 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13200278.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Geminia Matrona, daughter of Aulus, who lived an exceptionally long life of 120 years, marking her burial spot.

EDCS-13200279.translation: Geminia Pota lived for 40 years; she is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-13200279.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Geminia Pota, who lived 40 years, marking her burial place and wishing her bones well.

EDCS-13200280.translation: To the Divine Manes. Geminia Prima lived for 105 years, with her sons Smasala, who lived 20 years, Fidelis, who lived [missing number] years, Paulina, who lived 12 years, and Martialis, 8 years.
EDCS-13200280.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Geminia Prima, who lived 105 years, and also records the names and ages of her four children, Smasala, Fidelis, Paulina, and Martialis, suggesting a family tomb or monument.

EDCS-13200281.translation: To the Divine Manes. Geminia Procula Securi lived for 101 years. She left behind three grandsons from her son and four grandchildren from her daughter. She is buried here.
EDCS-13200281.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Geminia Procula Securi, noting her age of 101 and the number of grandchildren she left behind from both her son and daughter, indicating her burial location.

EDCS-13302570.translation: To the Divine Manes. Cannius Severus lived for 90 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13302570.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Cannius Severus, stating he lived 90 years and is buried in this spot.

EDCS-13302573.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Iulius Luci filius Quirina Iustus vixit annos LXXXI hic situs est
EDCS-13302573.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Julius Iustus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived for 81 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13302573.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Iulius Iustus, son of Lucius, identifying his tribe and stating he lived 81 years and is buried at this location.

EDCS-13600417.text_edition: Marco Valerio Maximiano Marci Valeri Maximiani quinquennalis sacerdotalis filio pontifici coloniae Poetovionensium equo publico praefecto cohortis I Thracum tribuno cohortis I Hamiorum civium Romanorum praeposito orae gentium Ponti Polemoniani donis donato bello Phartico sic allecto ab Imperatore Marco Antonino Augusto et misso in procinctu Germanicae expeditionis ad deducenda per Danuvium quae in annonam nnniae utriusque exercituum denavigarent praeposito vexillationum classium praetoriarum Misenatis item Ravennatis item classis Brittanicae item equitum Afrorum et Maurorum electorum ad curam explorationis Pannoniae praefecto alae I Aravacorum in procinctu Germanico ab Imperatore Antonino Augusto coram laudato et equo et phaleris et armis donato quod manu sua ducem Naristarum Valaonem interemisset et in eadem ala quartae militiae honorem adepto praefecto alae contariorum donis donato bello Germanico Sarmatico praeposito equitibus gentium nnum Naristarum Quadorum ad vindictam Orientalis motus pergentium honore centenariae dignitatis aucto salario adeptus procurationem Moesiae inferioris eodem in tempore praeposito vexillationibus et at sic detrahendam Briseorum latronum manum in confinio Macedoniae et Thraciae ab imperatore misso procuratori Moesiae superioris procuratori provinciae Daciae Porolissensis a sacratissimis impperatoribus in amplissimum ordinem inter praetorios allecto et mox legato legionis I adiutricis item legato legionis II adiutricis praeposito vexillationum Leugaricione hiemantium item legato legionis V Macedonicae item legato legionis I Italicae item legato legionis XIII geminae item legato Augusti pro praetore [legionis III Augustae] donis donato a nobilissimo [principe Marco Aurelio Commodo Augusto] expeditione secunda Germanica splendidissimus ordo Dianensium veteranorum aere conlato
EDCS-13600417.translation: To Marcus Valerius Maximianus, son of Marcus Valerius Maximianus the quinquennial priest, pontiff of the colony of Poetovio, public horseman, prefect of the First Thracian cohort, tribune of the First Cohort of Hamians, Roman citizens, commander of the coast of the nations of Pontus Polemonianus, awarded with gifts in the Parthian War, thus chosen by the Emperor Marcus Antoninus Augustus and sent on the eve of the Germanic expedition to lead down the Danube what was to be transported for the supply of both armies, commander of the detachments of the praetorian fleets of Misenum and Ravenna, and of the British fleet, and of selected African and Moorish cavalry for the reconnaissance of Pannonia, prefect of the First Aravacan Wing in the Germanic expedition, publicly praised by Emperor Antoninus Augustus and presented with a horse, phalerae, and arms because he had slain the Naristan chieftain Valao with his own hand, and having obtained the honor of fourth military service in the same wing, prefect of the Contarii Wing, awarded with gifts in the Germanic-Sarmatian War, commander of the cavalry of the Naristan and Quadian nations proceeding for the vengeance of the Eastern uprising, having been promoted to the honor of centenarian rank, having obtained the procuratorship of Lower Moesia, at the same time sent by the emperor as commander of detachments to suppress the band of Brician bandits on the border of Macedonia and Thrace, procurator of Upper Moesia, procurator of the province of Dacia Porolissensis, co-opted by the most sacred emperors into the highest order among the praetorians, and soon legate of Legio I Adiutrix, also legate of Legio II Adiutrix, commander of the detachments wintering in Leugaricio, also legate of Legio V Macedonica, also legate of Legio I Italica, also legate of Legio XIII Gemina, also legate of Augustus pro praetore of Legio III Augusta, awarded with gifts by the most noble prince Marcus Aurelius Commodus Augustus in the second Germanic expedition. The most splendid order of Dianensian veterans, by collected bronze, [set this up].
EDCS-13600417.summary: This is a highly detailed public inscription, likely a statue base, erected by the Dianensian veterans, commemorating the extensive military and civilian career of Marcus Valerius Maximianus, detailing his numerous commands, honors, and promotions under Emperors Marcus Antoninus Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Commodus Augustus, indicating a late 2nd to early 3rd-century AD date.

EDCS-13700376.text_edition: Herculi Augusto sacrum Marcus Iulius Cai filius Papiria Felicianus vir optimus praefectus pro IIviris quinquennalibus sacerdos Liberi Patris responso monituve eius sua pecunia cum base ceterisque omnibus ponsuit idemNque#idemque#IDENQUE victimis athibitis sic dedicavit curantibus Iulio Valerio Gaiano nepote et Rutilia Eugamia coniuge et Flavia Felicissima nuru et Iulia Septimina filia votum solvit libens animo  Posita III Idus Maias Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus
EDCS-13700376.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Marcus Iulius Felicianus, son of Gaius, of the Papirian tribe, an excellent man, prefect for the quinquennial duoviri, priest of Liber Pater, by his response or warning, set up this with his own money, along with the base and everything else. And he dedicated it with sacrifices. The dedication was overseen by Iulius Valerius Gaianus, his grandson, and Rutilia Eugamia, his wife, and Flavia Felicissima, his daughter-in-law, and Iulia Septimina, his daughter. He willingly fulfilled his vow. Placed on the 13th of May, in the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelianus.
EDCS-13700376.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar or monument to Hercules Augustus by Marcus Iulius Felicianus, a local official and priest, on behalf of himself and his family, dated to May 13, 237 AD.

EDCS-13700377.text_edition: Libero Patri et Liberae Auggustis sacrum signum Saturisci quem Iulia Urbana somnio praemonita promiserat Quintus Cominius Successus vir eius cum Cominiis Urbano Cominiano Cominia Quintiane sic filis eius reddere curaverunt idemque victimis athiAbitis sic#athibitis sic#ATHABITIS dedicaverunt votum solverunt libentes animo  Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus III Idus Maias posita
EDCS-13700377.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater and Libera Augusta. Iulia Urbana, having been forewarned in a dream, had promised this statue of Saturiscus. Her husband, Quintus Cominius Successus, with their sons Cominius Urbanus and Cominianus, and daughter Cominia Quintiana, ensured it was rendered. They likewise dedicated it with sacrifices and willingly fulfilled their vow. Placed on the 13th of May, in the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelianus.
EDCS-13700377.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Iulia Urbana, who, prompted by a dream, promised a statue of Saturiscus to Liber Pater and Libera Augusta, and its dedication by her husband and children, dated to May 13, 237 AD.

EDCS-15000064.inscription_type: tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-15000064.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Maximini Pii Felicis Augusti vexillarii legionis III Augustae piae vindicis Maximianae morantes castello Dimmidi sub cura Cai Ponti Aureliani |(centurionis) legionis eiusdem devoti numini eius aram Cerei consecratam V Nonas Maias Quintiano et Severo consulibus curantibus Marco Lisinio sic Cassiano stratore et Lucio Aurelio Emerito tesserario sculpsit Lucius Marius Namphamo immunis
EDCS-15000064.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the well-being of our lord, Imperator Caesar Gaius Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus. The vexillarii of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex Maximiana, residing at the fort of Dimmidi, under the care of Gaius Pontius Aurelianus, centurion of the same legion, devoted to his divinity, consecrated an altar to Cereus. This was done on the 5th of May, in the consulship of Quintianus and Severus. Overseeing were Marcus Lisinius Cassianus, strator, and Lucius Aurelius Emeritus, tesserarius. Lucius Marius Namphamo, immunis, sculpted it.
EDCS-15000064.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Cereus by vexillarii of Legio III Augusta at Dimmidi fort, for the well-being of Emperor Maximinus Thrax, dated to May 5, 235 AD.

EDCS-15200108.text_edition: ORI X Kalendas Martias Claudio Iuliano II et Bruttio Crispino consulibus
EDCS-15200108.translation: On the tenth day before the Kalends of March, in the consulship of Claudius Iulianus II and Bruttius Crispinus.
EDCS-15200108.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary date marker, recording a specific day in February, dated to 224 AD.

EDCS-15200125.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Agrippa Themi filius Palmyra qui fuit |(centurio) cohortis III Thracum Syria item translatus in cohortem I Chalcidenorum iusso sic Imperatoris curam egit Palmyrenorum sagittariorum nnorum X militavit nnos XXIII vixit annos LV es libertus et procurator
EDCS-15200125.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Agrippa, son of Themes, from Palmyra, who was a centurion of the Third Thracian Cohort in Syria. He was transferred by order of the Emperor to the First Cohort of Chalcideni and served as curator of Palmyrene archers for 10 years. He served for 23 years, lived for 55 years. Es, freedman and procurator.
EDCS-15200125.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Agrippa from Palmyra, detailing his military career as a centurion and curator of archers, who served for 23 years and died at age 55, set up by his freedman and procurator.

EDCS-15300545.text_edition: Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati filio patrono Publius Iulius Cai filius Papiria Iunianus
EDCS-15300545.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, Augustan legate pro praetore and consul-designate, our patron. Publius Iulius Iunianus, son of Gaius, of the Papirian tribe, [dedicates this].
EDCS-15300545.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Publius Iulius Iunianus to Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, son of a high-ranking official, recognizing him as a patron.

EDCS-15600011.text_edition: Silvano sancto Marcus Porcius Iustus praefectus legionis III Augustae posuit  Dedicata Idibus Ianuariis Bruttio Praesente II et Condiano consulibus
EDCS-15600011.translation: To Holy Silvanus, Marcus Porcius Iustus, prefect of Legio III Augusta, placed this. Dedicated on the Ides of January, in the consulship of Bruttius Praesens II and Condianus.
EDCS-15600011.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Silvanus Sanctus by Marcus Porcius Iustus, a legionary prefect, dated to January 13, 217 AD.

EDCS-16201702.text_edition: Marti Augusto sacrum pro salute dddominorum nnnostrorum Augggustorum Caius Egrilius Fuscianus beneficiarius Subatiani Proculi legati Augggustorum pro praetore consulis designati adiutor principis praetori scribatu Horati Viatoris et Didi Aprilis  Posita pridie Nonas Apriles Faustino et Rufino consulibus
EDCS-16201702.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus, for the well-being of our lords the Augusti. Gaius Egrilius Fuscianus, beneficiarius of Subatianus Proculus, Augustan legate pro praetore and consul-designate, assistant to the princeps, praetorian scribe for Horatius Viator and Didius Aprilis. Placed on the day before the Nones of April, in the consulship of Faustinus and Rufinus.
EDCS-16201702.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Mars Augustus by Gaius Egrilius Fuscianus, a beneficiarius and scribe, for the well-being of the emperors, dated to April 4, 210 AD.

EDCS-16201720.text_edition: Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore filio stratores
EDCS-16201720.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, Augustan legate pro praetore. The stratores [dedicate this].
EDCS-16201720.summary: This inscription is a dedication by cavalrymen (stratores) to Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, the son of a high-ranking provincial governor.

EDCS-16202031.text_edition: Marco Aemilio Macro Dinarcho Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore filio stratores
EDCS-16202031.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macer Dinarchus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, Augustan legate pro praetore. The stratores [dedicate this].
EDCS-16202031.summary: This inscription is a dedication by cavalrymen (stratores) to Marcus Aemilius Macer Dinarchus, the son of a high-ranking provincial governor.

EDCS-16300322.text_edition: Herculi Invicto sacrum pro salute et victoria Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Pii et Marci Aureli Antonini Auggustorum et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro in honorem Publi Aeli Quinti fili Meonia Menecratiani Zita ex |(centurione) legionis honesta missione patris sui permittente eo Publius Aelius Menecrates Florus eques primus pilus flamen perpetuus patriae suae donum dedit numero III
EDCS-16300322.translation: Sacred to Hercules the Unconquered, for the well-being and victory of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustuses, and of Julia Augusta, mother of Augustus. With the permission of Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augustuses with propraetorian power, a most distinguished man, and in honor of Publius Aelius, son of Quintus, of the Meonian tribe, Menecratianus Zita, a veteran of a century of the legion, whose honorable discharge was granted by his father, Publius Aelius Menecrates Florus, first equestrian centurion (primus pilus) and perpetual flamen of his native city, gave this gift number III.
EDCS-16300322.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Publius Aelius Menecrates Florus to Hercules the Unconquered for the well-being and victory of the Severan emperors (Lucius Septimius Severus and Caracalla) and Julia Augusta, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16400403.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo votum rettuli Genio Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Claudi Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti Aelius Dubitatus miles cohortis VIIII praetoriae centuria Etrii annis VIIII gessi stationem Veneria Rusicade salvis et felicibus commanipulis faciendum curavi
EDCS-16400403.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, I fulfilled a vow. To the Genius of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, Aelius Dubitatus, a soldier of the 9th Praetorian Cohort, from the century of Etrius, having served for 9 years, I performed duty at the station of Veneria Rusicade. With my comrades safe and happy, I ensured this was made.
EDCS-16400403.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of Emperor Claudius Gothicus, made by Aelius Dubitatus, a praetorian soldier, commemorating his service and wishing for the well-being of his comrades, likely dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-16400589.text_edition: Iovi omnipotenti sacrum Quintus Rufilius Quinti filius Arnensi Saturninus aedilis augur praefectus pro IIviris statuam quam ob honorem auguratus sui super legitimam ex SS V milibus nummum cum base promiserat ampliata pecunia posuit dedicavitque curantibus Rutilis Montano fratre et Ianuario et Saturnino filiis eius  Mamertino et Rufo consulibus Kalendis Decembribus
EDCS-16400589.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Omnipotent. Quintus Rufilius Saturninus, son of Quintus, of the Arnensian tribe, aedile, augur, prefect for the duumvirs, erected and dedicated this statue with its base, having promised it for the honor of his augurate beyond the legitimate sum, from 5,000 sesterces, with an increased sum. His brothers Rutilus Montanus and Januarius, and his sons Saturninus, Mamertinus, and Rufus oversaw it. On the Kalends of December, during the consulship of Mamertinus and Rufus.
EDCS-16400589.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Jupiter Omnipotent by Quintus Rufilius Saturninus, a municipal official, commemorating his augurate and funded with an amplified sum, dating specifically to December 1st of 227 AD.

EDCS-16400629.text_edition: Pro felicitate et incolumitate saeculi dominorum nnn(ostrorum) Auggg(ustorum) Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Parthici Britannici Germanici maximi Auggustorum et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti nostri castrorumque tubicines legionis III Augustae piae vindicis quorum nomina subiecta sunt  Antonius Proculus optio Valerius Felix princeps Aemilius Crescens Flavius Felix Annaeus Priscus Tuccius Fortunatus Gellius Rogatus Iulius Messianus Flavius Iulianus ilius Primus ndicius Sallustianus Valerius Castus ius Speratus Manilius Saturninus ius Fortunatus lius Venustus Caius Iulius Victor Antistius Successus  Quintus Granius Crispinus Quintus Fabius Donatus Caelius Faustus Postumius Urbanus Caius Annius Lucius Geminius Marcus Valerius Quintus Sulpicius Rutilius  Caius Vereius I Caius Secundius Lucius Cassius Fortunatus Flavius Rogatus Iulius Datus Pompeius Datus Domitius Ci Servilius  Aurelius D  Gellius Rosinus Caius Domitius Valens Aurelius Bassus  Scamnari nomine dabunt collegae qui facti fuerint |(denarios) DCCL anulari nomine instituimus veteranis missione accepta in singulos |(denarios) D vel si quis de collegis profecerit per ordinem accipiet |(denarios) D item si quis de collegis tramare profecerit cum praescripto accipiet viaticum pro merito |(denarios) CC eque arca D
EDCS-16400629.translation: For the good fortune and safety of the age of our lords the Augustuses, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus, and of Julia Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the camps: the trumpeters of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Vindicator, whose names are listed below: Antonius Proculus (optio), Valerius Felix (princeps), Aemilius Crescens, Flavius Felix, Annaeus Priscus, Tuccius Fortunatus, Gellius Rogatus, Julius Messianus, Flavius Julianus, Primus, Sallustianus, Valerius Castus, Speratus, Manilius Saturninus, Fortunatus, Venustus, Caius Julius Victor, Antistius Successus, Quintus Granius Crispinus, Quintus Fabius Donatus, Caelius Faustus, Postumius Urbanus, Caius Annius, Lucius Geminius, Marcus Valerius, Quintus Sulpicius, Rutilius, Caius Vereius, Caius Secundius, Lucius Cassius Fortunatus, Flavius Rogatus, Julius Datus, Pompeius Datus, Domitius, Servilius, Aurelius, Gellius Rosinus, Caius Domitius Valens, Aurelius Bassus. In the name of Scamnari, colleagues who shall be elected will give 750 denarii. In the name of the 'anular,' we have established for veterans who have received honorable discharge 500 denarii each, or if any of the colleagues is promoted through the ranks, he will receive 500 denarii. Likewise, if any of the colleagues is promoted by 'tramare' with the pre-established condition, he will receive a travel allowance according to merit of 200 denarii from the chest.
EDCS-16400629.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the trumpeters of Legio III Augusta for the well-being of the Severan imperial family (Lucius Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Julia Augusta) and establishes a fund for their colleagues and veterans, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16800380.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto  Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore Titus Aemilius Capitolinus
EDCS-16800380.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus as legate of Augustus with propraetorian power. Titus Aemilius Capitolinus.
EDCS-16800380.summary: This inscription records a dedication or monument, mentioning the emperor Caracalla and his legate Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, with Titus Aemilius Capitolinus as the dedicator, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-18300270.translation: Titus Flavius Bitus, a horseman of the 2nd Thracian Cohort Gemella, lived 55 years and served for 27 years. Julia Marcella, his sister, and Bitus, his son, made this.
EDCS-18300270.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Titus Flavius Bitus, a Thracian cavalryman who served for 27 years, erected by his sister Julia Marcella and his son Bitus.

EDCS-20100184.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli VII sic patri patriae Publius Metilius Secundus legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-20100184.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 7th time, father of his country. Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, patron of the colony, dedicated this by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-20100184.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Emperor Hadrian by Publius Metilius Secundus, a provincial governor and patron of the colony, funded by public money through a decurional decree, dating to around 121 AD.

EDCS-20100218.text_edition: Publio Iulio Iuniano Martialiano clarissimo viro consuli legato Augustorum pro praetore provinciae Numidiae proconsuli provinciae Macedoniae praefecto aerari militaris curatori viae Clodiae praetoriae tribuno plebei sic quaestori provinciae Asiae patrono coloniae et municipi res publica coloniae Thamugadensium decreto decurionum
EDCS-20100218.translation: To Publius Julius Junianus Martialis, most distinguished man, consul, legate of the Augustuses with propraetorian power for the province of Numidia, proconsul of the province of Macedonia, prefect of the military treasury, curator of the Clodian Road, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Asia, patron of the colony and municipality. The Republic of the colony of Thamugadi (Timgad) dedicated this by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20100218.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the colony of Thamugadi to Publius Julius Junianus Martialis, honoring his extensive senatorial career and patronage of the colony, likely dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-20300015.text_edition: Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici fili divi Commodi fratris divi Antonini nepotis divi Hadriani pronepotis divi Traiani Parthici abnepotis divi Nervae adnepotis Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimique principis pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis VII imperatoris XI consulis II patris patriae proconsulis et Imperatoris Caesaris Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi felicissimique principis fili divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepotis divi Antonini pronepotis divi Hadriani abnepotis divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepotis [] tribuniciae potestatis bis proconsulis Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimi felicissimique principis iuventutis Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti [fratris Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris Augusti] dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augg[gustorum] pro praetore consule amplissimo patrono coloniae et Saevinio Proculo tribuno laticlavio curatore rei publicae decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-20300015.translation: For the well-being of Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the divine Commodus, grandson of the divine Antoninus, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most brave prince, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, hailed Imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, proconsul; and of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most fortunate prince, grandson of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, holding tribunician power twice, proconsul; and of Emperor Caesar Publius Septimius Geta, noblest Caesar, brother of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augustuses with propraetorian power, most distinguished consul and patron of the colony, and Saevinius Proculus, laticlavian tribune, curator of the republic, by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-20300015.summary: This inscription records a public dedication for the health and well-being of the Severan imperial family, including Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta (whose name was later erased), by Quintus Anicius Faustus and Saevinius Proculus, funded by public money, dating to around 199 AD.

EDCS-20300016.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinaci Augusto Pio Felici fortissimoque principi Arabico Adiabenico dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augusti pro praetore designato consule clarissimo viro possessori vici Lambafundensium faciendum curaverunt pecunia conlata quorum nomina at latus basis inscripta sunt Laterano et Rufino consulibus
EDCS-20300016.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius Felix, and most brave prince Arabicus Adiabenicus. Dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, consul-designate, most distinguished man, and owner of the vicus of Lambafundensium. Those whose names are inscribed on the side of the base ensured this was made with collected money, during the consulship of Lateranus and Rufinus.
EDCS-20300016.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus, initiated by Quintus Anicius Faustus and funded by collected money from individuals whose names were on the base, dating to 207 AD.

EDCS-20300045.text_edition: Pro salute Imperatorum Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Publi Septimi Getae totiusque domus divinae et victoria Quinti Anici Fausti legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati vexillatio legionis III Augustae piae vindicis  morantes in procinctu sub cura Fontei Fortunati decurionis alae I nnoniorum Severo et Pompeiano consulibus Numisius Gemellus consulibus supra scriptis Caecilius Lucianus Flacco et Gallo consulibus Marcus An Saturninus Pisone et Iuliano consulibus Longinius Triton Pollione et Apro consulibus Iulius Verecundus Imperatore et Vero consulibus Valerius
EDCS-20300045.translation: For the well-being of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta and their entire divine house, and for the victory of Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, consul designate, a vexillation of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Avenging, remaining in procinctu, under the care of Fonteius Fortunatus, decurion of the First Ala of Thracians, in the consulship of Severus and Pompeianus, Numisius Gemellus in the aforementioned consulship, Caecilius Lucianus in the consulship of Flaccus and Gallus, Marcus Annius Saturninus in the consulship of Piso and Julianus, Longinius Triton in the consulship of Pollio and Aper, Julius Verecundus in the consulship of Imperator and Verus, Valerius...
EDCS-20300045.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by a vexillation of Legio III Augusta for the well-being and victory of the Severan imperial family, listing various individuals by the consulships during which they likely served or contributed, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300061.translation: To Victoria Augusta, for the well-being of our lords Valerian and Gallienus, Pious and Fortunate Augusti, soldiers of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Avenging, restored from Raetia, Gemini, returned on the 11th Kalends of November in the consulship of Volusianus II and Maximus, fulfilled their vow through Marcus Flavius Valens, centurion of the aforesaid legion. Lucius Volumnius Crescens, optio principis; Marcus Aurelius Licinius, optio; Gaius Geminius Victor, optio. Donatus sculpted and wrote this.
EDCS-20300061.summary: This inscription is a vow fulfilled by soldiers of Legio III Augusta upon their return from Raetia, dedicated to Victoria Augusta for the well-being of Emperors Valerian and Gallienus, dating to October 22, 253 CE.

EDCS-20300067.text_edition: Imperatores Caesares Marcus Aurelius Antoninus et Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Germanici Sarmatici fortissimi amphitheatrum vetustate corruptum a solo restituerunt per cohortem VI Commagenorum Aulo Iulio Pompilio Pisone Laevillo legato Augustorum pro praetore curante Aelio Sereno praefecto
EDCS-20300067.translation: The Emperors Caesars Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, Germanicus Sarmaticus, most valiant, restored the amphitheater, which had been ruined by age, from its foundations through the Sixth Cohort of Commagenians, with Aulus Julius Pompilius Piso Laevillus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, overseeing, and Aelius Serenus as prefect.
EDCS-20300067.summary: This inscription records the restoration of an amphitheater from its foundations by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Commodus, executed by the Cohors VI Commagenorum under the supervision of the provincial legate and a prefect, likely dating to the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20300073.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Antonino Augusto burgum speculatorum Antoninianorum Marcus Valerius Senecio legatus eius pro praetore clarissimus vir fieri iussit curam agente Caio Iulio Aelurione |(centurione) legionis III Augustae Antoninianae praeposito numero Hemesenorum Antoniniano
EDCS-20300073.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Augustus, Marcus Valerius Senecio, his legate with propraetorian power, a most distinguished man, ordered the construction of the burgus of the Antoninian Speculators, with Gaius Julius Aelurionus, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion Antoniniana, in charge as praepositus of the Hemesenian auxiliary unit.
EDCS-20300073.summary: This inscription commemorates the construction of a burgus (fortified tower) for the Antoninian Speculators, ordered by the provincial legate Marcus Valerius Senecio under the reign of Caracalla, with a centurion overseeing the work, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300074.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Commodo Antonino Pio Felice Augusto Germanico Sarmatico Britannico patre patriae tribunicia potestate XIII consule V burgum Commodianum speculatorium inter duas vias ad salutem commeantium nova tutela constitui iussit Tiberius Claudius Gordianus vir clarissimus legatus Augusti pro praetore cura agente
EDCS-20300074.translation: In the reign of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Britannicus, Father of the Country, in his 13th tribunician power, 5th consulship, Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, a most distinguished man, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, ordered the Commodian Speculators' burgus to be established as a new protection between two roads for the safety of travelers, with a cura agent overseeing.
EDCS-20300074.summary: This inscription records the establishment of the 'Commodian Speculators' burgus' by Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, the provincial legate, for the safety of travelers, during the reign of Emperor Commodus, dating to 188 CE.

EDCS-20300078.text_edition: Silvano Augusto sacrum Claudius Gordianus legatus Augusti pro praetore restituit
EDCS-20300078.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus. Claudius Gordianus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, restored this.
EDCS-20300078.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument dedicated to Silvanus Augustus by Claudius Gordianus, the provincial legate, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300080.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXI imperatori II consuli IIII patri patriae Lucio Matuccio Fuscino legato Augusti pro praetore legio III Augustae
EDCS-20300080.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, in his 21st tribunician power, 2nd imperatorial acclamation, 4th consulship, Father of the Country. Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power. The Third Augustan Legion.
EDCS-20300080.summary: This inscription is a dedication made by Legio III Augusta to Emperor Antoninus Pius, commemorating his titles and powers, and mentioning the provincial legate Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, dating to 158 CE.

EDCS-20300108.text_edition: Genio legionis III Augustae piae vindicis pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Felicis Parthici Britannici Germanici maximi Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro consule designato Titus Arranius Datus signifer ex HS III milibus nummum de suo posuit
EDCS-20300108.translation: To the Genius of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Avenging, for the well-being of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Felix Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus Augustus, and of Julia Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the camps, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, most distinguished man, consul designate, dedicating it. Titus Arranius Datus, a standard-bearer, paid 3,000 sesterces from his own money.
EDCS-20300108.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of Legio III Augusta for the well-being of the Severan imperial family, made by a standard-bearer, Titus Arranius Datus, and dedicated by the provincial legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300109.text_edition: Genio Lambaesis pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Felicis Parthici Britannici Germanici maximi Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matri Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro consule designato Lucius Baebius Faustianus signifer legionis III Augustae piae vindicis Luci Baebi Felicis veterani ex signifero filius votum solvit
EDCS-20300109.translation: To the Genius of Lambaesis, for the well-being of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Felix Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus Augustus, and of Julia Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the camps, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, most distinguished man, consul designate, dedicating it. Lucius Baebius Faustianus, standard-bearer of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Avenging, son of Lucius Baebius Felix, a veteran from a standard-bearer, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20300109.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Lucius Baebius Faustianus, a standard-bearer, to the Genius of Lambaesis for the well-being of the Severan imperial family, dedicated by the provincial legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300110.text_edition: Genio castrorum legionis III Augustae pro salute et incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Impperatorum Carini et Numeriani Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit devotus [numini maies]tatique eius
EDCS-20300110.translation: To the Genius of the camp of the Third Augustan Legion, for the well-being and safety of our lords, the Emperors Carinus and Numerianus. Marcus Aurelius Decimus, most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, formerly princeps peregrinorum, fulfilled his vow, devoted to their divinity and majesty.
EDCS-20300110.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, governor of Numidia, to the Genius of the legionary camp for the well-being of Emperors Carinus and Numerianus, dating to the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300111.text_edition: I patri pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Impperatorum Carini et Numeriani Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit
EDCS-20300111.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus (Iuppiter Pater) for the health and safety of our lords the Emperors Carinus and Numerian, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, formerly chief of the peregrini, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20300111.summary: This inscription is a votive offering dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a provincial governor, for the well-being of Emperors Carinus and Numerian, likely dating to the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20300115.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Traiano Hadriano Augusto fortissimo liberalissimoque dedicante Publio Cassio Secundo legato Augusti pro praetore veterani legionis III Augustae qui militare coeperunt
EDCS-20300115.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, most brave and most generous, with Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, dedicating, the veterans of Legio III Augusta who began their military service.
EDCS-20300115.summary: This monument was dedicated to Emperor Hadrian by veterans of Legio III Augusta, likely commemorating their service or a special grant, under the auspices of Publius Cassius Secundus, sometime during Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-20300117.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepote divi Nervae pro nepote Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio imperatore II pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VIIII consule IIII patre patriae Caio Prastina Messalino legato Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae cohors I
EDCS-20300117.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, acclaimed Emperor for the second time, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the ninth time, consul for the fourth time, father of his country, with Gaius Prastina Messalinus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, Legio III Augusta, Cohort I.
EDCS-20300117.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from a monument or building project, involving the First Cohort of Legio III Augusta, under the provincial governor Gaius Prastina Messalinus, dated to 146 AD based on the imperial titles.

EDCS-20300124.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV consuli IV patri patriae dedicante Marco Valerio Etrusco legato Augusti pro praetore Lucius Sempronius Ingenuus primipilaris
EDCS-20300124.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the fifteenth time, consul for the fourth time, father of his country, with Marcus Valerius Etruscus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, Lucius Sempronius Ingenuus, chief centurion.
EDCS-20300124.summary: This dedication honors Emperor Antoninus Pius and was erected by Lucius Sempronius Ingenuus, a primipilaris, with Marcus Valerius Etruscus acting as legate, dated to 152 AD based on the imperial titles.

EDCS-20300127.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Germanico patri patriae munimenta et turres a solo refectae per legionem III Augustam Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-20300127.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus, father of his country: the fortifications and towers were rebuilt from the ground up by Legio III Augusta, with Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power.
EDCS-20300127.summary: This inscription records the reconstruction of fortifications and towers by Legio III Augusta for Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus), under the supervision of legate Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, likely during the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20300131.text_edition: Imperatoris Caesaris divi Marci Antonini Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Pii Antonini nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis VI imperatori XI consuli II patri patriae proconsuli et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti nostri filio et fortissimo principi iuventutis Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti imperatoris nostri filio Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini fratri et Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro consule designato equites legionis III Augustae piae vincicis
EDCS-20300131.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Pius Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the sixth time, acclaimed Emperor for the eleventh time, consul for the second time, father of his country, proconsul; and to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our Augustus Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax, and to the most brave prince of youth, son of our Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, brother of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus; and to Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camps, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, a most distinguished man, consul designate: the cavalrymen of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex.
EDCS-20300131.summary: This inscription, erected by cavalrymen of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex, dedicates a monument to Emperor Septimius Severus, his son Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), and Julia Domna, with Quintus Anicius Faustus serving as legate, dated to 198 AD by the imperial titles.

EDCS-20300132.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo et Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Augusti nostri filio Parthico Britannico Germanico maximo Augusti nostri Antonini Felicis filio domini nostri Severi et Iuliae Augustae matri Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augggustorum pro praetore consule designato cornicularius et beneficiarii tribuni laticlavi milites legionis III Augustae piae vindicis ex arca sua fecerunt quorum nomina subiecta sunt Marcus Annius Honoratus cornicularius Caius Arrius Ingenuus Lucius Duronius Felix Caius Licinius Silvanus Caius Gabinius Vitalis Marcus Aemilius Rufinus Quintus Sutorius Secundus Geminius Ianuarius Lucius Cornelius Serapio Quintus Iulius Rufinus Caius Aelius Faustus Marcus Aemilius Catulus Sextus Caecilius Felix
EDCS-20300132.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, and to Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our Augustus, Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus, son of our Augustus Antoninus Felix, our lord Severus, and to Julia Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the camps, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, consul designate: a cornicularius and beneficiarii of the laticlavian tribunes, soldiers of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex, made this from their own fund, whose names are listed below: Marcus Annius Honoratus (cornicularius), Gaius Arrius Ingenuus, Lucius Duronius Felix, Gaius Licinius Silvanus, Gaius Gabinius Vitalis, Marcus Aemilius Rufinus, Quintus Sutorius Secundus, Geminius Ianuarius, Lucius Cornelius Serapio, Quintus Iulius Rufinus, Gaius Aelius Faustus, Marcus Aemilius Catulus, Sextus Caecilius Felix.
EDCS-20300132.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a cornicularius and beneficiarii of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex, listing their names, to Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, and Empress Julia Domna, under legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20600003.translation: For the good fortune and safety of the age of our lords the Augusti, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and [Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar] Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the camps, [and Fulvia Plautilla Augusta] and of the Senate and of the fatherland, [wife] of our Unconquered Antoninus Augustus: the horn players of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex. [Followed by a list of names and financial regulations for a collegium of horn players, stating that 750 denarii will be given by new colleagues, travel expenses of 200 denarii plus 500 from the fund, 500 denarii to veterans for a ring, 500 denarii if one is promoted to a higher rank, 500 denarii to heirs upon death, and 250 denarii if one loses their position; those who paid from the fund and new recruits will receive what is owed. The law was made on August 22nd, with Plautianus for the second time and Geta for the second time as consuls. Antoninus Filinus Marcus.]
EDCS-20600003.summary: This extensive inscription is a dedication by the horn players (cornicines) of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex to the Severan imperial family (Severus, Caracalla, Geta, Julia Domna, and Plautilla), which also details the internal financial regulations and benefits of their collegium, dated to August 22, 203 AD.

EDCS-20600026.publication: CIL 08, 02579a-c (p 954) = CIL 08, 18089a-c = D 03841a-c = Horster p 424 = LBIRNA 00198 = Hygiae p 121
EDCS-20600026.translation: To Jupiter Valens, these temples. To Aesculapius and Salus. Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus. They made this for Silvanus through Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-20600026.summary: This inscription records the construction or dedication of temples to Jupiter Valens, Aesculapius, Salus, and Silvanus by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, carried out by Legio III Augusta, likely during their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-20600029.text_edition: Iovi Valenti Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus Lucius Stertinius Rufinus legatus Augustorum pro praetore consul designatus
EDCS-20600029.translation: To Jupiter Valens, Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, legates of the Augusti with propraetorian power, consuls designate.
EDCS-20600029.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Valens by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, who were joint legates and consuls designate, indicating a joint provincial administration or a specific shared project.

EDCS-20600030.text_edition: Silvano Pegasiano Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus Lucius Stertinius Rufinus legatus Augustorum pro praetore consul designatus
EDCS-20600030.translation: To Silvanus Pegasianus, Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, legates of the Augusti pro praetore, consuls designate.
EDCS-20600030.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Pegasianus by two legates, Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, who were also designated consuls.

EDCS-20600038.translation: To Aesculapius the Holy, Marcus Porcius Iustus, prefect of the camp of the Third Augustan Legion, gave this gift. Dedicated on the Ides of November, in the consulship of Imperator Commodus III and Burrus.
EDCS-20600038.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aesculapius Sanctus by Marcus Porcius Iustus, a camp prefect of the Legio III Augusta, dated to 183 CE.

EDCS-20600039.text_edition: Hygiae Titus Caunius Priscus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus cum Vera uxore et Firmino et Prisca filiis
EDCS-20600039.translation: To Hygia, Titus Caunius Priscus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, consul designate, with his wife Vera and his children Firminus and Prisca.
EDCS-20600039.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hygia by Titus Caunius Priscus, legate and consul designate, along with his wife and children.

EDCS-20600040.text_edition: Aesculapio et Hygiae Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus vir clarissimus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore consul designatus
EDCS-20600040.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygia, Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a most distinguished man, legate of the Augusti pro praetore, consul designate.
EDCS-20600040.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aesculapius and Hygia by Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a distinguished legate and consul designate.

EDCS-20600042.text_edition: Monitu Apollinis Publius Metilius Secundus legatus Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-20600042.translation: By the warning of Apollo, Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-20600042.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Publius Metilius Secundus, a legate, at the behest of an oracle from Apollo.

EDCS-20600060.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo tempestatium divinarum potenti legio III Augusta dedicante Quinto Fabio Catullino legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-20600060.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, lord of divine storms, the Third Augustan Legion dedicates this, with Quintus Fabius Catullinus as legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-20600060.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, god of storms, made by the Legio III Augusta under the command of legate Quintus Fabius Catullinus.

EDCS-20600061.text_edition: Ventis bonarum tempestatium potentibus legio III Augusta dedicante Quinto Fabio Catullino legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-20600061.translation: To the powerful winds of good weather, the Third Augustan Legion dedicates this, with Quintus Fabius Catullinus as legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-20600061.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the beneficial winds by the Legio III Augusta, under the supervision of legate Quintus Fabius Catullinus.

EDCS-20600066.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque diis deabusque immortalibus Caius Macrinius Decianus vir clarissimus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Numidiae et Norici Bavaribus qui adunatis IIII regibus in provinciam Numidiam inruperant primum in regione Millevitana iterato in confinio Mauretaniae et Numidiae tertio Quinquegentaneis gentilibus Mauretaniae Caesariensis item gentilibus Fraxinensibus qui provinciam Numidiam vastabant capto famosissimo duce eorum caesis fugatisque
EDCS-20600066.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and all other immortal gods and goddesses, Caius Macrinius Decianus, a most distinguished man, legate of the Augusti pro praetore for the provinces of Numidia and Noricum, for the Bavarians who, after uniting four kings, invaded the province of Numidia, first in the Millevitana region, again in the borderland of Mauretania and Numidia, and a third time by the Quinquegentanei, tribesmen of Mauretania Caesariensis, and also by the Fraxinenses tribesmen who were devastating the province of Numidia, their most famous leader having been captured, and they themselves slain and put to flight.
EDCS-20600066.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and other deities by Caius Macrinius Decianus, a legate, commemorating Roman victories over several invading tribes in Numidia.

EDCS-20600076.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno  Publius Aelius Claudia Col Iprimus pilus legionis Volubili III Augustae
EDCS-20600076.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Publius Aelius Claudia Col Iprimus, first centurion of the Volubilis Third Augustan Legion.
EDCS-20600076.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Publius Aelius Claudia Col Iprimus, a first centurion of the Legio III Augusta.

EDCS-20600081.text_edition: Isidi et Serapi Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus legatus Augusti pro praetore aedem cum Volteia Cornificia uxore et Matuccia Fuscina filia ab antecessoribus suis institutam exaltatam et adiecto pronao per legionem III Augustam columnis sua pecunia positis exornavit
EDCS-20600081.translation: To Isis and Serapis, Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, together with his wife Volteia Cornificia and daughter Matuccia Fuscina, embellished the temple, which was initiated by his predecessors, raised, and had a pronaos added by the Third Augustan Legion, by placing columns at his own expense.
EDCS-20600081.summary: This inscription details the renovation and embellishment of a temple dedicated to Isis and Serapis by Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, a legate, along with his family, who financed the addition of columns to a structure initiated by his predecessors.

EDCS-20600085.text_edition: Deo Marti militiae potenti statuam in honorem legionis III Augustae Valerianae Gallienae Valerianae Sattonius Iucundus primus pilus qui primus legione renovata aput sic aquilam vitem posuit votum dedit dedicante Veturio Veturiano viro clarissimo legato Auggustorum pro praetore
EDCS-20600085.translation: To the God Mars, powerful in war, Sattonius Iucundus, primus pilus, who first placed the eagle and vine-staff in the legion when it was renewed at this place, dedicated this statue in honor of the Legio III Augusta Valeriana Galliena Valeriana, fulfilling a vow, with Veturio Veturiano, vir clarissimus, legate of the Augusti pro praetore, dedicating it.
EDCS-20600085.summary: This inscription records a statue dedicated by a primus pilus to Mars and the Legio III Augusta, fulfilling a vow upon the legion's renewal, with a consular legate officiating the dedication.

EDCS-20600088.text_edition: Pro salute Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii et senati sic populi Romani et Fuscini legati clarissimi viri et legionis III Augustae et auxilis eius Catius sacerdos Mauris de sua pecunia et locum instituit quos coli sub Statiano legato et Septiminus AV
EDCS-20600088.translation: For the well-being of the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, and of the Senate and Roman People, and of Fuscinus, vir clarissimus, legate, and of the Legio III Augusta and its auxiliaries, Catius, a priest of the Mauri, established this place at his own expense for their worship under Statianus, legate, and Septiminus AV.
EDCS-20600088.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a cult place for the Mauri by a priest named Catius, at his own expense, for the well-being of the emperor, the Roman state, and military forces, likely during the reign of Caracalla.

EDCS-20600089.translation: For the well-being of our Lord Severus Alexander, Pius, Felix, Augustus, to the Moorish Gods, Marcus Porcius Iasuctan, centurion of the Legio XX Valeria Victrix Severiana, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20600089.summary: This inscription records a centurion's fulfillment of a vow to the Moorish Gods for the well-being of Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-20600091.translation: Sacred to the Moorish Gods. Lucius Purtisius Firmus, a hastatus, with his wife Popilia Marciane, willingly made this.
EDCS-20600091.summary: This inscription records a dedication made to the Moorish Gods by Lucius Purtisius Firmus, a soldier, and his wife Popilia Marciane.

EDCS-20600092.translation: To the Moorish Gods Barbares, Servilius Impetratus willingly fulfilled his vow. I came as victor, may you make me a victor!
EDCS-20600092.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Servilius Impetratus to the Moorish Gods Barbares, likely seeking continued success in battle.

EDCS-20600094.text_edition: Deo Mercurio sancto Augusto Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae votum solvit libens animo feliciter
EDCS-20600094.translation: To the holy and august God Mercury, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, vir perfectissimus, governor of the province of Numidia, willingly and successfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20600094.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to Mercury by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a governor of Numidia.

EDCS-20600111.text_edition: Aquam Titulensem quam ante annos plurimos Lambaesitana civitas interverso ductu vi torrentis amiserat perforato monte instituto etiam a solo novo ductu Severinius Apronianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae patronus coloniae restituit curante Aelio Rufo viro egregio flamine perpetuo curatore rei publicae
EDCS-20600111.translation: The Titulensian water, which the city of Lambaesis had lost many years ago due to the diverted course and force of a torrent, was restored by Severinius Apronianus, vir perfectissimus, governor of the province of Numidia and patron of the colonia, by piercing a mountain and establishing a new channel from its foundation, with Aelius Rufus, vir egregius, perpetual flamen and curator rei publicae, overseeing the work.
EDCS-20600111.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a water supply to the city of Lambaesis by the provincial governor, Severinius Apronianus, with the help of a local official, after it had been lost to a torrent.

EDCS-20600178.text_edition: Moenia quisque dolet nova condere successori inculto maneat lividus hospitio Acilius Clarus vir consularis praeses provinciae Numidiae sibi et successoribus fecit
EDCS-20600178.translation: Let whoever grieves to build new walls for his successor remain envious in his uncultivated dwelling. Acilius Clarus, vir consularis, governor of the province of Numidia, made this for himself and his successors.
EDCS-20600178.summary: This inscription records the construction of new city walls by Governor Acilius Clarus, who boasts about his work and perhaps expresses a sentiment against those who might not appreciate his efforts.

EDCS-20600179.text_edition: Marco Aemilio Macro Dinarcho Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati filio patrono Publius Iulius Cai filius Papiria Iunianus
EDCS-20600179.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macer Dinarchus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, legate of Augustus pro praetore, consul designate, patron: Publius Iulius, son of Gaius, of the Papirian tribe, Iunianus.
EDCS-20600179.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Aemilius Macer Dinarchus, son of a high-ranking official and himself a patron, made by Publius Iulius Iunianus.

EDCS-20600247.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its specific content or purpose.

EDCS-20800596.text_edition: Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore filio Titus Valerius Rufinus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae
EDCS-20800596.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, imperial legate with propraetorian power. Titus Valerius Rufinus, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, [set this up].
EDCS-20800596.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a centurion to the son of an imperial legate, likely honoring the family of a provincial governor.

EDCS-20800597.text_edition: Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano clarissimo viro legato Auggustorum pro praetore omnibus virtutibus abundanti viro Memmius Valerianus a IIII militiis vir egregius
EDCS-20800597.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, most distinguished man, imperial legate with propraetorian power, a man abounding in all virtues. Memmius Valerianus, a man of exceptional rank, from his four military posts, [set this up].
EDCS-20800597.summary: This inscription is a dedication by an equestrian officer to an esteemed imperial legate, likely a provincial governor, praising his virtues.

EDCS-20800598.text_edition: Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro beneficiari eius curante Publio Valerio Donato quaestore
EDCS-20800598.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, most distinguished man. His beneficiaries [set this up], with the care of Publius Valerius Donatus, quaestor.
EDCS-20800598.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the beneficiaries of an imperial legate, likely a provincial governor, overseen by a quaestor, honoring the legate.

EDCS-20800601.text_edition: Publius Caelius Optatus legatus Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae restitui iussit
EDCS-20800601.translation: Publius Caelius Optatus, imperial legate with propraetorian power of the Third Augustan Legion, ordered [this] to be restored.
EDCS-20800601.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument or structure by a provincial governor, who was also the legate of the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-20800602.text_edition: Caio Fabio Fabiano Vetilio Luciliano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli designato praesidi iustissimo Aulus Tullius Iustianus primus pilus legionis III Augustae Severianae
EDCS-20800602.translation: To Gaius Fabius Fabianus Vetilius Lucilianus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, most distinguished man, consul-designate, most just governor. Aulus Tullius Iustianus, primus pilus of the Severan Third Augustan Legion, [set this up].
EDCS-20800602.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a senior centurion (primus pilus) to an distinguished imperial legate and future consul, highlighting his just governorship and military affiliation.

EDCS-20800603.text_edition: Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano Lucio Stertinio Rufino legato Augustorum pro praetore consuli designato Sextus Terentius Saturninus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae
EDCS-20800603.translation: To Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, consul-designate. Sextus Terentius Saturninus, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, [set this up].
EDCS-20800603.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a centurion to an imperial legate and consul-designate, likely a provincial governor, indicating support from the military.

EDCS-20800604.text_edition: Numisiae Publi filiae Celerinae coniugi Decimi Fontei Frontiniani legati Augustorum pro praetore consulis designati Magnia Procula mater Caecili Concessi corniculari
EDCS-20800604.translation: To Numisia Celerina, daughter of Publius, wife of Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, consul-designate. Magnia Procula, mother of Caecilius Concessus, cornicularius, [set this up].
EDCS-20800604.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the wife of an imperial legate and consul-designate, made by the mother of a military clerk (cornicularius), possibly acknowledging the legate's family.

EDCS-20800607.text_edition: Publio Iulio Iuniano Martialiano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli praesidi iustissimo et benignissimo Caius Calventius Ianuarius |(centurio) legionis III Augustae Alexandrianae
EDCS-20800607.translation: To Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialis, imperial legate with propraetorian power, most distinguished man, consul, most just and most benevolent governor. Gaius Calventius Ianuarius, centurion of the Alexandrian Third Augustan Legion, [set this up].
EDCS-20800607.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a centurion to an imperial legate, consul, and provincial governor, praising his justice and benevolence, and referencing the legion's epithet.

EDCS-20800608.text_edition: Tiberio Iulio Pollieno Auspici consulari patrono Sinicii Rufus et Fortunatus fratres advocati
EDCS-20800608.translation: To Tiberius Iulius Pollienus Auspex, ex-consul, patron of Sinicii. Rufus and Fortunatus, brothers, advocates, [set this up].
EDCS-20800608.summary: This inscription is a dedication by two advocate brothers to an ex-consul, acknowledging his patronage over a group named Sinicii, likely a local community or association.

EDCS-20800610.text_edition: Statiae Agrippinae coniugis Modi Iusti legati Augusti pro praetore consulis speculatores et beneficiari
EDCS-20800610.translation: To Statia Agrippina, wife of Modius Iustus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, consul. The scouts and beneficiaries [set this up].
EDCS-20800610.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the wife of an imperial legate and consul, set up by military scouts and beneficiaries, possibly acknowledging the legate's authority and patronage.

EDCS-20800611.text_edition: Lucio Novio Crispino Martiali Saturnino consuli designato legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Africae proconsuli Galliae Narbonensis legato Augusti legionis I Italicae legato Augusti iuridico Astyriae et Gallaeciae praetori tribuno plebis quaestori pro praetore provinciae Macedoniae tribuno militum legionis VIIII Hispanae IIIIviro viarum curandarum seviro equitum Romanorum veterani legionis III Augustae qui militare coeperunt Glabrione et Torquato item Asiatico II et Aquilino consulibus
