EDCS-24800676.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a statue to Fortuna Augusta by Caius Antonius Alexander, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, in gratitude for being elected perpetual flamen in absentia, with the monument dedicated under the authority of Lucius Novius Crispinus.

EDCS-24800693.text_edition: Minervae Augustae sacrum dedicante Lucio Novio Crispino legato Augusti pro praetore Quintus Iulius Quinti filius Quirina Martialis Cirta curiae Hadrianae felici veteranorum legionis III Augustae ob honorem flaminatus perpetui quem in se absentem contulerunt ex HS IIII milibus nummum ampliata pecunia praeter legitimam summam posuit
EDCS-24800693.translation: Sacred to Minerva Augusta. Dedicated by Lucius Novius Crispinus, imperial legate pro praetore. Quintus Iulius, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, Martialis, from Cirta, of the fortunate Hadriana curia of the veterans of the Third Augustan Legion, on account of the honor of perpetual flaminate which they conferred upon him in his absence, set this up from 4,000 sesterces, augmenting the legitimate sum.
EDCS-24800693.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Minerva Augusta by Quintus Iulius Martialis, a veteran from Cirta, in gratitude for being elected perpetual flamen in absentia, with the monument dedicated under the authority of Lucius Novius Crispinus.

EDCS-24800717.translation: Constantine, we always see your enemies thus enraged, Augustus, and we enjoy peace when in this Genius the province shows itself. For he orders crosses to be set up and the savage battles of the tyrant.
EDCS-24800717.summary: This is a laudatory inscription to Emperor Constantine, celebrating his victories over enemies and the peace he brought to the province, possibly alluding to his triumph over a tyrant.

EDCS-24800724.text_edition: Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore claro et inlustri viro
EDCS-24800724.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, imperial legate pro praetore of the Augusti, a distinguished and illustrious man.
EDCS-24800724.summary: This is a dedication honoring Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, an imperial legate of senatorial rank, for his distinguished public service.

EDCS-24900486.translation: Buita and Etopta give 550 sestertii for the Quintian and Geminian rites, from the workshop, as gifts. D(ecimus) L(ucius) Iund(ius)? In the 283rd year of the province.
EDCS-24900486.summary: This inscription records a donation by Buita and Etopta for religious rites, possibly from a workshop, made in the 283rd year of a Roman province.

EDCS-25001001.text_edition: Quintus Pompeius Cottinus beneficiarius vixit annis XXXXVIII hic situs est
EDCS-25001001.translation: Quintus Pompeius Cottinus, a beneficiarius, lived for 48 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001001.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Pompeius Cottinus, a beneficiarius, who lived to be 48 years old.

EDCS-25001002.text_edition: Memoriae Aureliae Salviae filiae piissimae quae vixit annis III mensibus X diebus VI Salvius pater
EDCS-25001002.translation: To the memory of Aurelia Salvia, a most dutiful daughter, who lived for 3 years, 10 months, 6 days. Salvius, her father.
EDCS-25001002.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Salvius, a father, to the memory of his most dutiful daughter, Aurelia Salvia, who died young.

EDCS-25001003.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Veneriae filiae dulcissimae Hilaritas mater eius vixit annis XXI mensibus XVI
EDCS-25001003.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. To Veneria, a most sweet daughter. Hilaritas, her mother. She lived for 21 years and 16 months.
EDCS-25001003.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed by Hilaritas, a mother, for her most sweet daughter Veneria, who lived for 21 years and 16 months.

EDCS-25001004.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Magniae Naidi feminae rarissimae uxori karissimae castissimae piissimae annos XLVIII menses VIII functae Campius dulcissimae maritus
EDCS-25001004.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. To Magnia Naidis, a most exceptional woman, my dearest, most chaste, and most dutiful wife, who lived for 48 years and 8 months. Campius, her most sweet husband.
EDCS-25001004.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed by Campius, a husband, for his most cherished, chaste, and dutiful wife, Magnia Naidis, who lived for 48 years and 8 months.

EDCS-25001005.text_edition: Lucius Longeius Primus  [] vixit annos LXXXX hic situs est ipse sibi
EDCS-25001005.translation: Lucius Longeius Primus lived for 90 years. He is buried here. For himself.
EDCS-25001005.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Longeius Primus, who lived for 90 years and erected it for himself.

EDCS-25001006.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum  Italus vixit nnos XXIII  Strato vixit nnos XXXXV  domini servis bene merentibus fecerunt
EDCS-25001006.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Italus lived for 23 years. Strato lived for 45 years. Their masters made this for their well-deserving slaves.
EDCS-25001006.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed, erected by masters for their two well-deserving slaves, Italus and Strato, who lived for 23 and 45 years respectively.

EDCS-25001008.text_edition: Dis Manibus Lucius Peticius Datus beneficiarius obita morte vixit annos XXXV hic situs
EDCS-25001008.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Peticius Datus, a beneficiarius, died and lived for 35 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001008.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed for Lucius Peticius Datus, a beneficiarius, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-25001009.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Tertia vixit annis LXII filius matri carissimae de suo posuit et dedicavit
EDCS-25001009.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Tertia lived for 62 years. Her son placed and dedicated this from his own means for his dearest mother.
EDCS-25001009.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed by a son, who placed it from his own means for his dearest mother, Iulia Tertia, who lived for 62 years.

EDCS-25001010.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Liciniae Geminae uxori vixit annos XLV Publius S T maritus fecit
EDCS-25001010.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. To Licinia Gemina, my wife, who lived for 45 years. Publius S. T., her husband, made this.
EDCS-25001010.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed by Publius S. T., a husband, for his wife, Licinia Gemina, who lived for 45 years.

EDCS-25001011.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Caecilius Castrensis se vivo fecit
EDCS-25001011.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Caecilius Castrensis made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-25001011.summary: This is a funerary inscription made by Caecilius Castrensis for his own tomb during his lifetime.

EDCS-25001013.translation: To Victoria, from Arborius the father.
EDCS-25001013.summary: This is a dedication made by Arborius the father to the goddess Victoria, likely on a small votive offering.

EDCS-25001014.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelius Exuperans vixit annos LXV menses VI sepulchrum Iulia Extricata marito dulcissimo fecit cum vixit [vi?]xit et semper omnes aluit
EDCS-25001014.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Exuperans lived for 65 years and 6 months. Julia Extricata made this tomb for her sweetest husband, with whom she lived and who always provided for everyone.
EDCS-25001014.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Exuperans, erected by his wife Julia Extricata, highlighting his marital relationship and role as a provider.

EDCS-25002174.text_edition: Cereri matri et filiae Augustis sacrum Caius Bottius Luci filius Quirina Saturninus Abdus Maior aram cum altaribus votum solvit libens animo
EDCS-25002174.translation: Sacred to Ceres, Mother and Daughter, Augustae. Gaius Bottius Saturninus Abdus Maior, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, willingly and with a grateful heart fulfilled his vow by dedicating an altar with altars.
EDCS-25002174.summary: This is a votive inscription made by Gaius Bottius Saturninus Abdus Maior, dedicated to Ceres Mater and Filia, indicating a fulfilled vow with the dedication of an altar.

EDCS-25500161.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed, to the memory of Gaius Fabius Clodianus, a most steadfast jurist.
EDCS-25500161.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Gaius Fabius Clodianus, described as a steadfast jurist.

EDCS-25500172.translation: To Annaedia Januaria, who lived 10 years.
EDCS-25500172.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Annaedia Januaria, recording her age at death as 10 years.

EDCS-25600185.text_edition: Imperatore domino nostro Gordiano Augusto et Aviola consulibus Decimus N Victor magister et Messius PicatinusI#Picatinus#PICATINI collega pridie Kalendas Apriles
EDCS-25600185.translation: In the consulship of our lord Emperor Gordian Augustus and Aviola, Decimus N. Victor, master, and Messius Picatinus, colleague, on the day before the Kalends of April (March 31st).
EDCS-25600185.summary: This inscription records a date, March 31st, during the consulship of Emperor Gordian and Aviola, and names two officials, Decimus N. Victor and Messius Picatinus.

EDCS-25600201.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the house. For the well-being of Quintus Antistius Adventus Postumus Aquilinus, Augustan legate of the Second Adiutrix Legion, and Novia Crispina his wife, and Lucius Antistius Mundicus Burrus, and Antonia Prisca his mother, and their children and household, Antistius Agathopus, a freedman, gave this gift in accordance with a vision. Quintus Antistius Agathopus gave this gift in accordance with a vision and dedicated it on the Kalends of March (March 1st) in the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus. Sacred to the Genius of the house. For the well-being of Quintus Antistius Adventus Postumus Aquilinus, Augustan legate of the Second Adiutrix Legion, and Novia Crispina his wife, and Lucius Antistius Mundicus Burrus, and Antonia Prisca his mother, and their children and household, Agathopus, a freedman, gave this gift in accordance with a vision. Quintus Antistius Agathopus gave this gift in accordance with a vision and dedicated it on the Kalends of March (March 1st) in the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus.
EDCS-25600201.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Genius of the house, made by the freedman Antistius Agathopus for the well-being of his former master Quintus Antistius Adventus Postumus Aquilinus, his family, and household, dated to the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus (217 CE).

EDCS-25600400.text_edition: Lucio Apronio Pio legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli designato patrono coloniarum ob insignem erga cives benivolentiam et iustitiam eius res publica Siguitanorum
EDCS-25600400.translation: To Lucius Apronius Pius, Augustan legate propraetor, most distinguished man, consul designate, patron of the colonies, in recognition of his outstanding benevolence and justice towards the citizens, the Republic of the Siguitani.
EDCS-25600400.summary: This is an honorific inscription by the Republic of the Siguitani for Lucius Apronius Pius, praising his benevolence and justice as a patron and high-ranking official.

EDCS-25600401.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti agri publici Siguitanorum  agri divisi Siguitanis
EDCS-25600401.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, Augustan legate, the public lands of the Siguitani, divided lands for the Siguitani.
EDCS-25600401.summary: This inscription records the division of public land among the Siguitani, authorized by Publius Cassius Secundus, an Augustan legate.

EDCS-25600402.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti agri publici Siguitanorum  agri accepti Cirtensium
EDCS-25600402.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus, the public land of the Siguitani, land accepted from the Cirtensians.
EDCS-25600402.summary: This inscription records a land transaction concerning public land of the Siguitani that was acquired from the Cirtensians, conducted under the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, an Augustan legate, likely dating to the early-mid 2nd century AD.

EDCS-25600403.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti agri publici Siguitanorum  agri divisi Siguitanis
EDCS-25600403.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus, the public land of the Siguitani, land divided among the Siguitani.
EDCS-25600403.summary: This inscription records the division of public land among the Siguitani people, carried out under the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, a legate of Augustus, likely dating to the early-mid 2nd century AD.

EDCS-25600621.text_edition: Res publica Verecundensium dedicante Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato
EDCS-25600621.translation: The Republic of the Verecundenses, with Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, consul designate, dedicating.
EDCS-25600621.summary: This inscription records an act by the Republic of the Verecundenses, likely the dedication of a monument, with Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, a consular legate of Augustus, officiating, probably in the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600722.translation: To the Divine Manes. Decimus Calvinius [starts here...]
EDCS-25600722.summary: This is a funerary inscription, dedicated to the Divine Manes, for Decimus Calvinius, but the full text is incomplete, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-25601741.translation: Gaius Julius Severus, son of Gaius, of the Papirian voting tribe, from Theveste, pious, lived 13 years. Julius Valens, optiō, made this for his brother.
EDCS-25601741.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Severus, who lived 13 years, erected by his brother Julius Valens, an 'optio' (a military rank), likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-25601742.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Iulius Victor lived 11 years and 6 and a half hours. His parents made this.
EDCS-25601742.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Lucius Iulius Victor, who died at a very young age, erected by his grieving parents, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-25800014.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare divi Nervae filio Nerva Traiano Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IV consule III patre patriae Lucio Munatio Gallo legato Augusti pro praetore XIX
EDCS-25800014.translation: To Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland; Lucius Munatius Gallus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, 19 times.
EDCS-25800014.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Emperor Trajan, listing his imperial titles and dating it to around 101 AD, under the authority of his legate Lucius Munatius Gallus.

EDCS-25800017.translation: To the Emperors Caesars Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power, Father of the Fatherland for the 2nd time, consul, proconsul; and Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Volusianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, proconsul; the Republic of the colony of Thamagadi [made this].
EDCS-25800017.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the Republic of the colony of Thamagadi to the co-emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, listing their numerous titles and dating to their joint reign in 251-253 AD.

EDCS-25800075.translation: To Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland; Lucius Munatius Gallus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, 17 times.
EDCS-25800075.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Trajan, detailing his imperial titles and dating to approximately 101 AD, with Lucius Munatius Gallus acting as his pro-praetorian legate.

EDCS-25900013.text_edition: Caio Velleio Paterculo legato Augusti legionis III Augustae XXIX
EDCS-25900013.translation: To Gaius Velleius Paterculus, legate of Augustus, of the Third Augustan Legion, 29 times.
EDCS-25900013.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication to Gaius Velleius Paterculus, a legate of Augustus commanding the Legio III Augusta, likely dating to the 1st century AD.

EDCS-25900026.text_edition: via facta a Larcio Prisco legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-25900026.translation: The road was made by Larcius Priscus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power.
EDCS-25900026.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road by a Roman legate, Larcius Priscus.

EDCS-25900482.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patre patriae IIII et Marco Aurelio Caesare II consulibus per Prastinam Messalinum legatum Augusti pro praetore vexillatio legionis VI ferratae viam fecit
EDCS-25900482.translation: When Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country for the fourth time, and Marcus Aurelius Caesar for the second time were consuls, a detachment of the Sixth Ferrata legion built the road through Prastina Messalinus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power.
EDCS-25900482.summary: This inscription commemorates the construction of a road by a vexillation of the Legio VI Ferrata, overseen by the legate Prastina Messalinus, during the consulship of Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Caesar.

EDCS-25900488.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Publio Helvio Pertinace Augusto patre patriae tribunicia potestate consule II Lucio Naevio Quadratiano legato Augusti pro praetore a Lambaese milia passuum LVIIII
EDCS-25900488.translation: When Emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus, father of the country, holding tribunician power and consul for the second time, was proconsul, and Lucius Naevius Quadratianus was legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, from Lambaese it is 59 miles.
EDCS-25900488.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, indicates a distance of 59 miles from Lambaese, recording the imperial titles of Emperor Pertinax and the legate Lucius Naevius Quadratianus.

EDCS-25900492.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Publio Helvio Pertinace Augusto pro praetore tribunicia potestate consule II Lucio Naevio Quadratiano legato Augusti pro praetore a Lambaese milia passuum L
EDCS-25900492.translation: When Emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus was pro praetore, holding tribunician power and consul for the second time, and Lucius Naevius Quadratianus was legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, from Lambaese it is 50 miles.
EDCS-25900492.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, indicates a distance of 50 miles from Lambaese, recording the imperial titles of Emperor Pertinax and the legate Lucius Naevius Quadratianus.

EDCS-25900545.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti pontes viae novae Rusicadensis res publica Cirtensium sua pecunia fecit Sexto Iulio Maiore legato Augusti legionis III Augustae pro praetore
EDCS-25900545.translation: By the authority of Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, the Republic of Cirta built the bridges of the new Rusicadensian road with its own money, with Sextus Iulius Maior, legate of Augustus of the Third Augustan Legion, as propraetor.
EDCS-25900545.summary: This inscription records the construction of bridges for a new road by the Republic of Cirta, funded by them, under the authority of Emperor Hadrian and the supervision of legate Sextus Iulius Maior.

EDCS-26000980.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus, the Undefeated, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, and to Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Volusianus, the Undefeated, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, consuls.
EDCS-26000980.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusian, likely erected during their shared consulship in 252 CE.

EDCS-26100133.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonius Gallus, the Undefeated, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul; and to Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus, the Undefeated, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, father of the country, holding tribunician power, consul, proconsul; to you, the Undefeated Emperors, and to your households.
EDCS-26100133.summary: This inscription is a comprehensive dedication honoring the co-emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusian, listing their full imperial titles and extending reverence to their families.

EDCS-26100328.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Cominius Paliarius vixit annos LXXXI
EDCS-26100328.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Cominius Paliarius lived 81 years.
EDCS-26100328.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating the tomb to the spirits of the dead and commemorating Lucius Cominius Paliarius, who lived to be 81 years old.

EDCS-26100341.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Clodius Urbanus vixit annos LXXV hic situs est
EDCS-26100341.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Clodius Urbanus lived 75 years; he lies here.
EDCS-26100341.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating the tomb to the spirits of the dead and commemorating Marcus Clodius Urbanus, who lived to be 75 years old and is buried at this spot.

EDCS-26100343.text_edition: Lucius Cominius Quetus vixit annos LXXXXII
EDCS-26100343.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Lucius Cominius Quetus, stating he lived for 92 years.

EDCS-27300292.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani nepote divi Nervae pronepote Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio consule IIII tribuniciae potestatis XXI Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus patronus coloniae decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-27300292.translation: Under Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of divine Hadrian, grandson of divine Trajan, great-grandson of divine Nerva, in his fourth consulship, in the 21st year of his tribunician power, Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, imperial legate with propraetorian power, consul designate, patron of the colony, [set this up] by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-27300292.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public work or monument funded by public money, initiated by Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, a high-ranking official, during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius (c. 157-158 CE).

EDCS-27300294.text_edition: et onerari se inlicitis --- militum atque officialium exactionibus  decreti concili quod suscitavitquerelas cum magno animi mei dolore audivi temporum illorum quorum  fuit ad nunc quis aequo animo ferat  exactionibus inlicitis quibus  imponunt fortunis alienis imminere ruinam m exauriant compendis suis uam populi vel fisci debiti I procere quis non et milites  parentium ac liberorum  summa excipit officiales munificentia  ne quasi quodam more constituto  publici vectigalis paterentur  ne posthac admittant aut poenae iis prodelicti qualitate inrogentur s de qua re et proccuratoribus meis litteras misi et rescriptum meum etiam provincialibus innotescere volui ciant Lucius Apronius Pius legatus Augusti [] IIIA eius circa provinciam suam hic
EDCS-27300294.translation: And to be burdened by unlawful [exactions] of soldiers and officials, concerning the decree of the council, which gave rise to complaints, I heard with great pain of my soul about those times, of which it has been until now, who would calmly endure the unlawful exactions by which they impose ruin on others' fortunes, or exhaust with their own gains, the debt of the people or the treasury. Who would not both the soldiers and parents and children, with the greatest generosity, receive the officials, lest, as if by some established custom, they suffer public taxation, lest they admit such things hereafter, or penalties be inflicted upon them according to the quality of the offense. Concerning which matter, I have also sent letters to my procurators, and I wished my rescript to be known to the provincials. Let them know, Lucius Apronius Pius, imperial legate, in his third [term] concerning his province here.
EDCS-27300294.summary: This inscription is a rescript from an emperor (likely Antoninus Pius, given the EDCS context) addressing and condemning the unlawful exactions and abuses by soldiers and officials against provincials, indicating the emperor's intention to enforce penalties and make his ruling widely known, likely issued through his legate Lucius Apronius Pius.

EDCS-27600035.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, in his third acclamation as imperator, in his 19th year of tribunician power, his fourth consulship, father of the country, restored [this]. 15.
EDCS-27600035.summary: This inscription records a restoration project undertaken by Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Augustus) around 216 CE.

EDCS-27600254.translation: To our Lord Flavius Crispinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-27600254.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Flavius Crispus, the son of Constantine the Great, referring to him as 'most noble Caesar' (c. 317-326 CE).

EDCS-27600278.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-27600278.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, acknowledging his titles (c. 284-305 CE).

EDCS-27600282.translation: Under the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus, Augustus, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-27600282.summary: This inscription records an event or construction project that took place during the reign of Emperor Maximian (c. 286-305 CE).

EDCS-27600283.translation: Under the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Maximianus, Augustus, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-27600283.summary: This inscription records an event or construction project that took place during the reign of Emperor Maximian (c. 286-305 CE).

EDCS-27600327.translation: Under the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Pious, Fortunate, Undefeated Augustus, again consul.
EDCS-27600327.summary: This inscription records an event or construction project that occurred during the second consulship of Emperor Diocletian (c. 284-305 CE).

EDCS-27900240.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator IV consul V pater patriae Lucio Minicio Natale legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-27900240.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, son of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, in his 9th year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator 4 times, in his 5th consulship, father of the country, [set this up] to Lucius Minicius Natalis, imperial legate with propraetorian power.
EDCS-27900240.summary: This inscription is a dedication made by or in honor of Emperor Trajan, referencing his titles and a legate named Lucius Minicius Natalis, likely dating to around 105-106 CE.

EDCS-28000969.text_edition: Bombia Sexti filia Martila vixit annos XXXX hic sita est
EDCS-28000969.translation: Bombia Martila, daughter of Sextus, lived for 40 years. She lies here.
EDCS-28000969.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for a woman named Bombia Martila, recording her name, filiation, age, and burial place.

EDCS-28000970.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Brinnia Iucunda vixit annos CXXXV hic sita eL(st)#e(st)#L
EDCS-28000970.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Brinnia Iucunda lived 135 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-28000970.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Brinnia Iucunda, noting her age and burial place.

EDCS-28000971.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Horatia Senecionis filia Monnula vixit annos L hic sita est
EDCS-28000971.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Horatia Monnula, daughter of Senecio, lived 50 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-28000971.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Horatia Monnula, daughter of Senecio, stating her age and burial location.

EDCS-28000973.text_edition: RO CI hic sit- est ossa tibi bene quiescant Dis Manibus
EDCS-28000973.translation: RO CI, here he/she is buried. May your bones rest well. To the Spirits of the Dead.
EDCS-28000973.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for an individual whose name is mostly lost, wishing them peaceful rest and invoking the Spirits of the Dead.

EDCS-28000997.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for an individual named Sentius, noting his burial.

EDCS-28200022.translation: The Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Parthicus Arabicus Adiabenicus Maximus, father of his country, with tribunician power for the [---]th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and Geta Caesar Augustus, our lords, his sons, [erected this] 10 miles from Tigava.
EDCS-28200022.summary: This is an honorific inscription, likely from a milestone, commemorating the Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), and Geta, and specifying a distance from Tigava, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200099.translation: The Imperator Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Invictus Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, proconsul.
EDCS-28200099.summary: This is an honorific inscription or a part of a milestone dedicated to Emperor Diocletian, detailing his full titulature and offices, dating to the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200132.translation: To our eternal lord, Imperator Constantius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, the public affairs of the colony of Lambaesis [erected this].
EDCS-28200132.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated by the colony of Lambaesis to the Emperor Constantius, likely Constantius II, probably from the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-38301833.text_edition: Domino orbis Invicto ac perpetuo principi domino nostro Flavio Iulio Constantio victori semper Augusto Flavius Ovidius Apthonius vir clarissimus consularis
EDCS-38301833.translation: To the lord of the world, the invincible and perpetual prince, our lord Flavius Iulius Constantius, ever victorious Augustus. Flavius Ovidius Apthonius, a most distinguished man, consular, [erected this].
EDCS-38301833.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Flavius Iulius Constantius (likely Constantius II), erected by Flavius Ovidius Apthonius, a consular, dating to the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-42700002.translation: In these properties of Aurelius Marcellinus, a most perfect man, dux ducum of victories, and Marcella, his most distinguished daughter, and Egyptilla, his most distinguished mother, [---].
EDCS-42700002.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely marking ownership or a dedication within the properties of Aurelius Marcellinus, a high-ranking official, mentioning his daughter and mother.

EDCS-43600026.translation: Up to this point, Aurelius Marcinus, a soldier from the province of Moesia Superior, who was killed in battle, lived 35 years, and served for 14 years. Aurelius Victor and Flavius Severianus erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-43600026.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Marcinus, a soldier from Moesia Superior who died in battle, erected by Aurelius Victor and Flavius Severianus, dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-43700621.text_edition: [Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius] [Commodus Antoninus] [Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus] [pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate ] [imperator  consul  pater patriae divi] Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Marco Valerio Maximiano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro VIII
EDCS-43700621.translation: To Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power [X times], imperator [Y times], consul [Z times], father of the country, son of divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of divine Pius, great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of divine Nerva. For Marcus Valerius Maximianus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, most distinguished man. (This is) the 8th (milestone).
EDCS-43700621.summary: This inscription is a milestone dedicated to Emperor Commodus, detailing his full titulature and lineage, and acknowledging Marcus Valerius Maximianus, his legate, likely dating to Commodus' reign (late 2nd century AD).

EDCS-43700685.translation: To Imperator Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus. The 4th (milestone).
EDCS-43700685.summary: This inscription is a milestone dedicated to Emperor Maximinus Thrax, listing his imperial titles, and indicating its position as the fourth unit along a road, dating to his reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-45600003.text_edition: Magniae Urbicae Augustae coniugi domini nostri Marci Aureli Carini Augusti matri castrorum ordo splendidissimus coloniae Lambaesitanorum devotus numminibus eorum dedicante Marco Aurelio Decimo viro perfectissimo praeside provinciae Numidiae
EDCS-45600003.translation: To Magnia Urbica Augusta, wife of our lord Marcus Aurelius Carinus Augustus, Mother of the Camps. The most splendid order of the colony of the Lambaesitani, devoted to their divinities, dedicated this through Marcus Aurelius Decimus, most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia.
EDCS-45600003.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the local council of Lambaesis to Magnia Urbica, empress and wife of Carinus, in her role as 'Mother of the Camps,' commissioned by the provincial governor, dating to the late 3rd century AD during Carinus' reign.

EDCS-46400007.translation: By Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, Marcus Aurelius Carinus, most noble Caesar, and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Numerianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, Germanicus maximus, Gothicus maximus, holding tribunician power, consuls, fathers of the country, proconsuls. From Tacapae, 39 miles.
EDCS-46400007.summary: This inscription is a milestone erected under the joint rule of Emperors Carus and Numerianus, and Carinus as Caesar, detailing their numerous titles and indicating a distance of 39 miles from Tacapae, dating to 283 AD.

EDCS-46700180.translation: To Liber Pater Augustus, by the will of Gaius Julius Victorinus, son of Gaius, of the Papiria tribe, quaestor, aedile, duovir, perpetual flamen. His sons, Julius Scaeva Crescentianus Victorinus, placed and dedicated this. Dedicated on the 16th day before the Kalends of April (March 17th), in the 187th year of the province.
EDCS-46700180.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Liber Pater Augustus, established by the will of Gaius Julius Victorinus and completed by his sons, dating to March 17th of the 187th year of the provincial era (likely 223 AD in Africa Proconsularis).

EDCS-46800201.translation: To our lord Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, most noble Caesar Augustus, son of our Imperator Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Augustus. From Calceus, 3 miles.
EDCS-46800201.summary: This inscription is a milestone dedicated to Maximinus Caesar, son of Emperor Maximinus Thrax, indicating a distance of 3 miles from Calceus, dating to Maximinus Thrax's reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-47501091.translation: Aelius Felix and Aelia Feliciana, his wife, and their daughter Vale[...], placed and dedicated this table on the 15th day before the Kalends of May (April 17th) in the 260th year of the province.
EDCS-47501091.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a table by Aelius Felix, Aelia Feliciana, and their daughter, dating to April 17th of the 260th year of the provincial era (likely 296 AD in Africa Proconsularis).

EDCS-47600546.text_edition: Neptuno sacrum Publius Cassius Sophron libertus et nomenclator Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati votum solvit
EDCS-47600546.translation: Sacred to Neptune. Publius Cassius Sophron, freedman and nomenclator of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, consul designate, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-47600546.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Neptune by Publius Cassius Sophron, a freedman associated with Publius Cassius Secundus, a high-ranking official, likely dating to the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-47600548.text_edition: Neptuno Augusto sacrum Bacchylus Titi Caeserni Statiani legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati actor votum solvit libens animo
EDCS-47600548.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Bacchylus, agent of Titus Caesernius Statianus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, consul designate, willingly and gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-47600548.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Neptune Augustus by Bacchylus, an agent for Titus Caesernius Statianus, a high-ranking official, likely dating to the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-47700002.translation: To the Divine Manes. Claudia Lucida, daughter of Lucius, pious, lived 7 years, is buried here. The first and most renowned of her family to all, whose grace and beauty and integrity of character are unsurpassed, dear to her husband, content with one husband. Alas, what a crime! Yet, joyful in her fate, she lived for 90 years. Thus dear, Iabucus Arnensis and Furius Iariega, to found and bury her bones.
EDCS-47700002.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Claudia Lucida, who died at age seven, and also commemorates an older woman who lived 90 years, likely buried by Iabucus Arnensis and Furius Iariega, dating generally to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-54800033.translation: Julius Maximus, soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, Antoniniana, made this tomb for himself while alive, at his own expense. He was about 54 years old.
EDCS-54800033.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Julius Maximus, a soldier, built a tomb for himself during his lifetime, and notes his age at the time of the inscription's creation or his death, likely around the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-58200007.translation: When Imperator Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus was consul for the second time, and Imperator Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius [Gallus Veldumianus] Voluss[i]anus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus was consul, 11 miles.
EDCS-58200007.summary: This inscription, dated to 252 AD, marks a distance of 11 miles and indicates the consuls in office at that time.

EDCS-59600010.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Parthico maximo Medico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXI imperatori V consuli III patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto Armeniaco Parthico maximo Medico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatori V consuli III patri patriae divi Antonini fili divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti [Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus legatus Augustorum pro praetore ] dedicavit
EDCS-59600010.translation: To Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Maximus Medicus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 21st year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator 5 times, consul 3 times, father of his country; and to Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Maximus Medicus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 7th year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator 5 times, consul 3 times, father of his country; son of divine Antoninus, grandson of divine Hadrian, great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of divine Nerva; Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, dedicated this.
EDCS-59600010.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to the co-emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, likely a statue base or an arch, by their legate Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, dating it to 166 AD.

EDCS-59600016.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Medico Parthico maximo imperatore V pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XXIII consule III patre patriae divi Antonini Pii filio divi Hadriani nepote divi Traiani Parthici pronepote divi Nervae abnepote plateam Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-59600016.translation: When Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Maximus was imperator for the 5th time, Pontifex Maximus, in his 23rd year of tribunician power, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, son of divine Antoninus Pius, grandson of divine Hadrian, great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of divine Nerva, Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, legate of the Augustus with propraetorian power, consul designate, patron of the colony, dedicated this square by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-59600016.summary: This inscription, dated to 168 AD, records the dedication of a public square by Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, a legate and patron of the colony, by decree of the local council and funded by public money.

EDCS-61800279.translation: To the most pious and victorious Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, the order of the colony of Lambaesis, devoted to his spirit and majesty, [dedicated this].
EDCS-61800279.summary: This inscription, likely for a statue base, is a dedication by the local council of Lambaesis to the Emperor Maximian, celebrating his piety and victories, dating to around 293-305 AD.

EDCS-63400138.inscription_type: tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-63400138.text_edition: $ pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Augusti et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae] totiusque domus divinae aram Cereri consecraverunt V Nonas Maias Manilio Fusco II et Domitio Dextro consulibus Titus Flavius Saturninus miles legionis supra scriptae inscripsit et sculpsit  Marcus  Lucius Iu Caius Annius Caius Valerius V Lucius Stertinius  Lucius Perelius Caius Iulius  Caius Grasidius Lucius Servilius x Caius Arruntius vi  Benius ns Iulius Iovinus Aurelius aiai cohortis secundae lius Donatus Caius Aelius Nervianus Lucius Cornelius Ianuarius Caius Aemilius Saturninus Caius Iulius Honoratus  Valerius v  Crispius Suc Marcus Aurelius Saturus Ma  Venius Consortinus Marcus Clodius Saturus Castra sig(nifer) Marcus Valerius Processus Cillio Caius Iulius Saturninus Castra    aen  Aug us Rogatianus Cillio cius Miscin cohortis X ius Fusc Castra Lucius Cilius Fortunatus Castra Lucilius Felix Karthagine Octavius Aemeritus sic Castra esius Antoninus Cillio  Aelius Fortunatus Cillio  Iulius Liberalis Cillio  Flavius Ianuarius Cillio item equitum  Iulius Donatus  Aemilius Lucianus  Iulius Saturn  Iulius Fundanus  Iulius Donatus  Flavius sitanus Quintus Clodius Vitalis thina  Geminius V  Aurelius Iius   Munatius Concordius
EDCS-63400138.translation: [For the safety of Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus and Julia Mamaea Augusta] and their entire divine house, this altar was consecrated to Ceres on the 5th day before the Nones of May, when Manilius Fuscus and Domitius Dexter were consuls. Titus Flavius Saturninus, a soldier of the above-written legion, inscribed and sculpted it. [Numerous names follow, likely a list of dedicants from military units, including centurions, signifiers, and soldiers from various cohorts and detachments, some from Castra (the fort) and others from Carthage or Cillium].
EDCS-63400138.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Ceres for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander and Julia Mamaea by a large group of soldiers from various units, likely from the legion stationed in Lambaesis, dated to 225 AD.

EDCS-66200098.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Anulius Pacatus vixit annos LXXXVI
EDCS-66200098.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Anulius Pacatus lived 86 years.
EDCS-66200098.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Quintus Anulius Pacatus and stating his age at death.

EDCS-66200099.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Caecilius Lupus vixit annos LXXXXV hic situs est
EDCS-66200099.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Caecilius Lupus lived 95 years; he lies here.
EDCS-66200099.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Caecilius Lupus, indicating his age at death and burial place.

EDCS-66300007.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepote Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate X consule III patre patriae decreto decurionum pecunia publica Sextus Iulius Maior legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit
EDCS-66300007.translation: When Imperator Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of divine Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, in his 10th year of tribunician power, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, was reigning, Sextus Julius Maior, legate of the Augustus with propraetorian power, patron of the colony, dedicated this by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-66300007.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument, likely a public work, by Sextus Julius Maior, a legate and patron of the colony, funded by public money and decreed by the local council, during the reign of Hadrian (126 AD).

EDCS-66300009.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo fortissimo max(imo) tribunicia potestate VII imperatore XI consule II proconsule divi Commodi fratre divi Pii Antonini nepote divi Traiani Parthici abnepote divi Nervae adnepote Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate II Publio Septimio Geta Caesare Augusto Augusti nostri fratre et Iulia Domna Augusta matre castrorum vetustate corruptas ampliato opere excoluit dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum patrono coloniae
EDCS-66300009.translation: When Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most brave, Maximus, in his 7th year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator 11 times, consul 2 times, proconsul, brother of divine Commodus, grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of divine Nerva; and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, in his 2nd year of tribunician power; and Publius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, brother of our Augustus; and Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camps, were reigning, he restored and enhanced works corrupted by age, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti and patron of the colony, dedicating.
EDCS-66300009.summary: This inscription records the restoration and enhancement of existing structures by Emperor Septimius Severus, his son Caracalla, and Geta Caesar, alongside Julia Domna, with the dedication performed by Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely dating to 194 AD.

EDCS-66300014.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Publio Helvio Pertinace Augusto patre patriae tribunicia potestate consule II Lucio Naevio Quadratiano legato Augusti pro praetore a Lambaese milia passuum
EDCS-66300014.translation: To the Imperator Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus, father of his country, with tribunician power, consul for the second time; and to Lucius Naevius Quadratianus, imperial legate with propraetorian power; from Lambaesis, X thousand paces.
EDCS-66300014.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Emperor Pertinax and his legate Lucius Naevius Quadratianus, likely marking a milestone or boundary marker, datable to 193 CE.

EDCS-67100103.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Salustius Urbanus, from the Second Flavian Cohort of Africans, lived for 50 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-67100103.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a tomb to Lucius Salustius Urbanus, a soldier of the Cohors II Flavia Afrorum, erected by an unspecified dedicant.

EDCS-67700020.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the name Probus, with insufficient context to determine its original purpose.

EDCS-67700086.summary: This is a highly fragmented inscription, making it impossible to determine its original meaning or context.

EDCS-69100011.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or record involving Carthaginians and mentioning Julia Apronia Saephare, possibly from a public monument or a tomb.

EDCS-69200168.translation: ...of the municipality of the Cedientes, columns... which they promised from their generosity... Victorinus, curator of the republic.
EDCS-69200168.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a public building project, possibly the erection of columns, for the municipality of Cedientes, funded by benefaction and overseen by Victorinus, a curator of the republic.

EDCS-70000001.translation: To Imperator Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Invictus, Pious, Felix, Augustus.
EDCS-70000001.summary: This is a monumental dedication to the Emperor Diocletian, likely an honorific inscription or part of a larger public work, datable to his reign (284-305 CE).

EDCS-70000022.translation: To Imperator Caesar, son of the divine Hadrian, grandson of the divine Trajan, great-grandson of the divine Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, with tribunician power for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, father of his country; the Mopthenses (dedicated this) with public money.
EDCS-70000022.summary: This honorific inscription, erected by the Mopthenses with public funds, is dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, datable to his 14th year of tribunician power and fourth consulate (150-151 CE).

EDCS-70100108.translation: To the Genius of the camp of the Third Augustan Legion, for the health and safety of our lords Valerius Diocletianus and Maximian.
EDCS-70100108.summary: This is a religious dedication to the Genius of the legionary camp, made for the well-being of the co-emperors Diocletian and Maximian, datable to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-70100123.translation: Imperator... Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius.
EDCS-70100123.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning an emperor, specifically Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, indicating it was likely an honorific dedication to Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-70100155.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Marcus Abadius Felix, standard-bearer of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Vindicatrix, lived for 40 years. Marcus Abadius Mar[---] made this for his deserving brother.
EDCS-70100155.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the 2nd or 3rd century AD dedicated by Marcus Abadius Mar[---] to his brother, Marcus Abadius Felix, a standard-bearer in the Roman army.

EDCS-74100033.translation: Naevia [---]ge lived [---] years. She lies here.
EDCS-74100033.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Naevia [---]ge, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-75100011.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Bottia Rogata vixit annos LXXXXV hic sita est
EDCS-75100011.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Bottia Rogata lived 95 years. She lies here.
EDCS-75100011.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Bottia Rogata, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-75100012.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Saturius Maximus vixit annos L hic situs est
EDCS-75100012.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Saturius Maximus lived 50 years. He lies here.
EDCS-75100012.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Lucius Saturius Maximus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-78300397.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, hailed as imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 6th time, with Lucius Acilius Strabo [and] Gaius Clodius Nummus as legates of Augustus pro praetore of the Third Augustan Legion.
EDCS-78300397.summary: This inscription records a dedication or public work undertaken in the name of Emperor Trajan, specifically mentioning his titles and the legates of Legio III Augusta, dating it precisely to 109 AD.
